coredata.dc:title: MRI-traceable multifunctional magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MMSN) capped with graphene quantum dots (GQD) as a theragnostic system in colorectal cancer treatment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Hojjati, Seyyed Mostafa
Salehi, Zeinab
Akrami, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: In the study, a multifunctional nanosystem composed of magnetic core-mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MMSN) capped with graphene quantum dots (GQD) was proposed as a theragnostic platform against colorectal cancer cells. The theragnostic carrier was well characterized and had an average particle size of 140 nm, with a specific surface of 475.82 m2 g−1. The superparamagnetic nanoparticles showed a hysteresis loop, with a maximum magnetization of 14.93 emu.g−1 and a remanence of 2.64 emu.g−1. The functionalization of MMSN was confirmed using the ninhydrin test. Regorafenib (REGO), an anticancer drug, was used with a loading capacity of 77.5 μg mg−1. The drug release profiles from the carrier and the sensitivity of the interaction between MMSN and GQD to pH were examined, revealing that more drug release was observed in acidic pH. The cytotoxicity of the MMSN-REGO/GQD system was evaluated on colorectal cancer cell line SW-480 using MTT assay, and the results indicated that no cytotoxicity was observed for the MMSN/GQD carrier. Furthermore, the IC50 of REGO was significantly reduced from 85 μg ml−1 to 6 μg ml−1 in the loaded form. The response of MMSN/GQD nanoparticles to the external magnetic field exhibited the capability of the theragnostic system as an MRI contrast agent. Generally, biocompatible, theragnostic, pH-responsive, multifunctional MMSN/GQD can be considered promising candidates for treatment, MRI imaging, and hyperthermia in colorectal cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots
Gatekeeper
pH-responsive
Theranostic system
Targeted drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MMSN)
REGO loading and GQD capping on MMSNs (MMSN-REGO/GQD)
Response of MMSN/GQD to external magnetic field
Response of MMSN/GQD to external magnetic field

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of MMSN-REGO/GQD nanoparticles and controlled drug release traceable with MRI.
Schematic of the synthesis steps of MMSN-REGO/GQD.
FESEM images of A) Fe3O4, B) MMSN, C) MMSN/GQD, and TEM images of D) MMSN, and E) MMSN/GQD.
A) N2 adsorption and desorption diagram, and B) BJH pore size distribution for MMSN.
XRD pattern for Fe3O4, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD, B) FTIR spectra for Fe3O4, MMSN, GQD, MMSN/GQD, REGO, and MMSN-REGO/GQD.
A) TGA, B) VSM diagram of Fe3O4, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD.
Images of Ninhydrin test A) before and B) after the reaction. Microtubes (a), (c) contain MMSN-NH2, microtubes (b), (d) contain MMSN, and (e) is ninhydrin/ethanol solution.
Cumulative percentage of REGO release from REGO-MMSN-NH2 and MMSN-REGO/GQD at pH 1.2 and 7.4.
Cell viability of MMSN/GQD, free REGO, and MMSN-REGO/GQD at different concentrations of drug.
TE-signal intensity diagrams in different concentrations of A) Fe3O4, B) MMSN, and C) MMSN/GQD.

A) T2 in different concentrations for Fe3O4, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD, B) Variation of 1/T2 versus concentrations for Fe3O4, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD. (The slope of the linear regression line for each sample was taken as the r2 value under the experimental conditions.)
Results of VSM analysis for Fe3O4, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD.

head.ce:title: MRI-traceable multifunctional magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MMSN) capped with graphene quantum dots (GQD) as a theragnostic system in colorectal cancer treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology & Pharmaceutical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology & Pharmaceutical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Biomaterials and Medical Biomaterials Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Biomaterials and Medical Biomaterials Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Biomaterials and Medical Biomaterials Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Biotechnology Lab., School of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, 16 Azar St., Tehran, Box: 1417614411, Iran.
Corresponding author. Pharmaceutical Biomaterial Lab, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Science, 16 Azar St., Tehran, Box: 141556451, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the study, a multifunctional nanosystem composed of magnetic core-mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MMSN) capped with graphene quantum dots (GQD) was proposed as a theragnostic platform against colorectal cancer cells. The theragnostic carrier was well characterized and had an average particle size of 140 nm, with a specific surface of 475.82 m2 g−1. The superparamagnetic nanoparticles showed a hysteresis loop, with a maximum magnetization of 14.93 emu.g−1 and a remanence of 2.64 emu.g−1. The functionalization of MMSN was confirmed using the ninhydrin test. Regorafenib (REGO), an anticancer drug, was used with a loading capacity of 77.5 μg mg−1. The drug release profiles from the carrier and the sensitivity of the interaction between MMSN and GQD to pH were examined, revealing that more drug release was observed in acidic pH. The cytotoxicity of the MMSN-REGO/GQD system was evaluated on colorectal cancer cell line SW-480 using MTT assay, and the results indicated that no cytotoxicity was observed for the MMSN/GQD carrier. Furthermore, the IC50 of REGO was significantly reduced from 85 μg ml−1 to 6 μg ml−1 in the loaded form. The response of MMSN/GQD nanoparticles to the external magnetic field exhibited the capability of the theragnostic system as an MRI contrast agent. Generally, biocompatible, theragnostic, pH-responsive, multifunctional MMSN/GQD can be considered promising candidates for treatment, MRI imaging, and hyperthermia in colorectal cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is one of the most prevalent and lethal malignancies worldwide, with a high mortality rate and poor prognosis [1]. Chemotherapy is the first-line treatment for CRC, including fluoropyrimidines, oxaliplatin, and irinotecan, as well as monoclonal antibodies such as bevacizumab, cetuximab and panitumumab and human recombinant fusion proteins, such as aflibercept [2]. However, it often causes severe side effects and drug resistance due to the non-specific distribution of cytotoxic agents and the heterogeneity of tumor cells [3]. Nevertheless, additional options are needed after disease progression or intolerance to standard therapies [4].

Regorafenib (REGO) is an orally bioavailable multi-kinase inhibitor with anticancer activity recommended for third-line treatment of CRC. It has been approved by the FDA in 2012 for the treatment of metastatic CRC [5]. REGO has shown significant antitumor activity and long-lasting antiangiogenic effects in preclinical models and clinical trials [6]. However, it also has a high toxicity profile, which limits its clinical use [7]. Consequently, there is an urgent need for advanced drug delivery systems or effective combined strategies to enhance therapeutic efficacy and reduce systemic toxicity. One promising approach is using theragnostics and multifunctional nanomaterials to perform diagnosis and therapy simultaneously [8]. These nanomaterials can provide real-time monitoring of drug delivery, biodistribution, pharmacokinetics, and therapeutic response, as well as targeted delivery of drugs or other therapeutic agents to tumor sites [9].

Nanomedicine has revolutionized drug production, and it has changed the patterns of therapy and tissue engineering due to the benefits of nanocarriers, such as high surface-to-volume ratio, unique features of surface modification, and engineering to produce particles in different sizes, shapes, and chemical properties. Recently, it has been proven that these particles are biocompatible, degradable, and non-toxic, making them ideal for medical and pharmaceutical applications [10]. If designed appropriately, nanoparticles might be able to target tissues or tumor cells as a drug delivery vehicle, preventing drug leakage during delivery [11].

Magnetic nanoparticles (MNP) are an essential category of nanoparticles typically made of pure metals (Ni, Co, Fe, and some rare earth metals) or a combination of metals and polymers. MNPs are utilized in various medical fields, such as hyperthermia therapy, controlled drug release, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and biosensors. The advantage of these nanoparticles is their capacity to be affected by an external magnetic field. The chemical composition, size, shape, morphology, and magnetic behavior of these nanoparticles are crucial in determining their biomedical applications. In addition, a safe and biocompatible coating can be applied to adjust the magnetic properties and effectiveness of MNPs within the body, making them more practical for a specific purpose. Surface chemistry modification can also lead to the multifunction of these nanoparticles [12].

Theragnostics is a treatment strategy that combines therapeutics with diagnostics. Theragnostic nanoparticles (TNP) can enhance the effectiveness of traditional medicines. These nanoparticles possess theragnostic properties and tumor-targeting capabilities in a single platform, resulting in an innovative and multifunctional system that can improve the speed and efficacy of therapy [13]. With TNPs, active and passive targeting can be performed simultaneously, and drug release can be controlled based on environmental characteristics. Molecular imaging can also be used in conjugation with other therapeutic methods. Achieving a practical synthesis of TNPs requires a balancing imaging sensitivity, targeting accuracy, and controlled drug release [14].

Multifunctional silica nanoparticles with uniform porosities, known as mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs), have recently gained popularity in drug delivery and cancer therapy. These nanoparticles offer distinct advantages, such as uniform and adjustable pore size, easy and independent surface, internal and external pores operation, and pore opening control mechanism. In recent years, scientists have successfully used MSNs to load various materials, from drugs to macromolecules like protein, DNA, and RNA [10]. For instance, Chen et al. designed a stimulus-responsive nanocarrier for intracellular drug delivery using nucleic acid attached to the MSN system. Thermal and DNase I enzyme methods have been investigated to control the drug release, and appropriate bioavailability, cellular uptake, and intracellular drug delivery have prepared the conditions for in vivo studies [15]. Controlled drug delivery can increase therapeutic efficiency and reduce the side effects of toxic drugs. However, drug resistance of cancer cells in chemotherapy is an inevitable and severe issue [16].

In recent years, studies have been conducted to improve cancer treatment by combining chemotherapy with other methods such as magnetic hyperthermia, photo-thermal therapy, gene therapy, and radiotherapy; therefore, efforts to produce multifunctional magnetic nanoparticle-incorporated MSN systems have increased [16]. For example, Lu et al. synthesized mesoporous silica nanocapsules with a magnetic core, which can generate heat by applying a magnetic field inside the body [17]. Besides, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have also shown remarkable success in various applications such as drug delivery, biosensors, bio-imaging, magnetic hyperthermia, photo-thermal therapy, antibacterial, catalyst, environmental protection, and energy. In this regard, Chen et al. capped GQDs on silica-coated nanoparticles through an acetal bond, enabling drug release from the porosity of the nanoparticles when the bond is broken in acidic conditions. The synthesis of this system aims to explore the potential use of intracellular drug delivery and imaging [18]. Furthermore, Zheng et al. fabricated innovative drug carriers based on fluorescence-enhanced energy transfer by coating GQDs on fluorescent MSNs via ATP aptamer, allowing for real-time ATP-based drug release control [19].

Although significant progress has been made in developing targeted drug delivery systems for anticancer drugs to specific sites, new nanomaterials are constantly being discovered to enhance drug delivery efficiency. A drug delivery system using GQD caps and drug carriers made of MSNs could be a multifunctional platform with tremendous potential in medical and pharmaceutical sciences [20]. Some scientists have developed core/shell materials that can be combined with photodynamic and thermodynamic therapy, offering promising drug delivery and release possibilities [21]. For instance, Yao et al. developed a multifunctional platform consisting of caps and local photo-thermal generators for drug release, magnetothermal therapy, and phototherapy. The caps used in this study are GQD nanoparticles, and the drug doxorubicin (DOX) is loaded in the pores of the mesoporous silica coating around the magnetic core made of Fe3O4. With the tests performed in laboratory conditions, it was found that this multifunctional system increases antitumor efficiency and holds great potential for antitumor treatment [22]. Similarly, Gao et al. synthesized multifunctional MSN with GQDs caps for drug delivery, photo-thermal therapy, and redox-responsive release in cancer tissue, resulting in the high drug release ability in a drug delivery controlled by external stimuli such as light or reduction [23]. Moreover, Ramedani et al. introduced a new type of fluorescent contrast agent based on GQDs attached to MSN. This study investigates this agent's potential to identify and sort cancer cells. The result is the ability of this system to effectively differentiate between cancer cells and normal cells and the prospect of using it in cancer diagnosis and therapy [24].

In this research, a multifunctional theragnostic nanosystem was developed based on iron nanoparticles coated with mesoporous silica and capped with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (Fig. 1 ). Iron nanoparticles were synthesized by combining Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions, coated by mesoporous silica nanoparticles around them. Coated nanoparticles were functionalized with APTES and capped with GQDs synthesized from graphene oxide sheets. REGO was loaded into the pores as the drug, characterized by physiochemical and theragnostic properties. The drug release was evaluated with and without the GQD caps to assess the interaction between MSN and GQDs, as well as the release behavior of the REGO. An MTT assay on CRC SW-480 cell lines was performed to investigate the cytotoxicity of the nanosystem. The diagnostic capability of the nanoparticles was evaluated by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The results demonstrated the potential of the nanosystem in the effective detection and drug release for theragnostic applications.

Most of the materials were required for this research, including iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), iron (II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), chloroform (CHCl3, 99 %), ammonia (NH3, 25 %), pure ethanol (C2H6O, 97 %), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3), orthophosphoric acid (H3PO4), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95–98 %), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), acetic acid (C2H4O2), sodium acetate (C2H3NaO2), oleic acid (C18H34O2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Merck, Germany. Medical ethanol (C2H6O, 70 %) was purchased from Mojallali, Iran. Graphite powder from Daye Jong, China, phosphate salt buffer tablet (DMF) from Tamadkala, Iran, and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, 99 %) from Thermofisher, America, were obtained. All the calculations have been done with the characteristics mentioned by the manufacturers, and no purification has been applied to them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MMSN)
ce:section.ce:para:
Magnetic iron nanoparticles were synthesized by the modified coprecipitation method [25]. Briefly, 100 ml of deionized water in a round-bottom three-necked flask was exposed to nitrogen gas for 30 min to remove the dissolved oxygen in the water. The amount of 0.78 g of FeSO4·7H2O and 1.44 g of FeCl3·6H2O (molar ratio 2:1) were added to the water. The solution was then placed on a magnetic stirrer at 80 °C and 500 rpm until the ions were completely dissolved. After that, the ammonia solution (25 %) was added dropwise to the flask under vigorous stirring (1000 rpm, 80 °C, 30 min), and the pH was adjusted to 11. At this time, the color of the solution changed from orange to black, which means the production of iron nanoparticles (black color). The stirring continued at 80 °C and 1000 rpm for 90 min, and during this time, 0.3 g of oleic acid was added dropwise to the flask to obtain the hydrophobic iron nanoparticles. Nitrogen gas was continuously blown into the solution inside the balloon in all synthesis steps. Finally, the resulting nanoparticles were separated from the solution by an external magnet, and after sequential washing with deionized water and ethanol (70 %) three times, they were dried at a temperature of 60 °C for 24 h. The reactions are as follows [26].
FeSO4·7H2O(s) → Fe2+ (aq) + SO4 2− (aq) + 7H2O(l)
FeCl3·6H2O(s) → Fe3+ (aq) + 3Cl- (aq) + 6H2O(l)
Fe
( aq )
2 +
+ 2
OH
( aq )
−
⇌
Fe
( OH )
2 ( s )
Fe
( aq )
3 +
+ 3
OH
( aq )
−
⇌
Fe
( OH )
3 ( s )
Fe
( OH )
3
( s )
⇌
FeOOH
( s )
+
H 2
O
( l )
2
FeOOH
( s )
+ Fe
( OH )
2
⇌
Fe 3
O
4 ( s )
+ 2
H 2
O
( l )
Fe
( aq )
2 +
+ 2
Fe
( aq )
3 +
+ 8
OH
( aq )
−
⇌
Fe 3
O
4
( s )
+ 4
H 2
O
( l )

The common method of synthesis by CTAB and TEOS was used to coat magnetic nanoparticles with silica nanoparticles [27]. Firstly, a solution containing 0.7 g of CTAB and 20 ml of deionized (DI) water was prepared as the aqueous phase. 1 mL of the chloroform solution containing 30 mg of iron oxide nanoparticles from the previous step was added to the aqueous phase as an organic phase. The solution was sonicated for 30 min at 50 °C to form a proper interaction between iron nanoparticles and CTAB. At this step, the color of the solution should be dark brown or black. Then the sonicated solution was placed at 70 °C for 15 min until the chloroform was evaporated entirely. The sample was then added to the solution containing 263 ml of DI water and 2.1 ml of NaOH 2 M and heated to 70 °C with stirring. After the temperature of the solution reached 70 °C, 3 ml of TEOS was added dropwise and slowly to the solution. The color of the solution at this stage should be milky. The solution was stirred for 2 h at a temperature of 70 °C, and then the resulting nanoparticles were separated with an external magnet. The final nanoparticles were washed three times with deionized water and three times with ethanol.

For removing the CTAB substrate and mesoporous silica coating, a solution containing 160 mg of NH4NO3 and 60 ml of ethanol (70 %) was made. Then, the nanoparticles obtained from the previous step were heated in NH4NO3 solution for 15 min under stirring. This step was repeated three times to remove CTAB completely. The resulting nanoparticles were washed 5 times with deionized water and dried at 60 °C for 24 h for further use.

The main goal of synthesizing GQDs is to cap and prevent drug leakage inside the pores and use heat emission to destroy cancerous tissue. In this regard, graphene oxide sheets (GO) were firstly synthesized according to the modified Hummers' method, and secondly, GQDs were synthesized from GO.

For the synthesis of GO by the modified Hummers' method, 1 g of graphite sheets was dispersed in H3PO4/H2SO4 (1:9) mixture. The prepared mixture was stirred for 30 min and heated to 50 °C. Then 6 g of KMnO4 was slowly added to the mixture for an hour and stirred overnight at 50 °C. Then an ice bath was created, and the temperature of the mixture was kept below 20 °C. 200 ml of frozen deionized water was gradually added to the mixture. 5 ml of hydrogen peroxide 30 % was added to the mixture, and the color change of the mixture to yellow at this step is a sign of the completion of the reaction. The final suspension was washed with hydrochloric acid 1 M to remove excess ions and acids from the reaction flask. Finally, the resulting GOs were washed several times with Ethanol/DI Water (1:5 v/v) solution and separated by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 20 min. The resulting particles were dried in an oven and stored [28].

To synthesize GQDs, the aqueous mixture containing GO was sonicated at room temperature for 30 min to homogenize the particles. 40 ml of H2O2 30 % was added to 3 ml of GO 8 mg/ml and placed on a stirrer at a temperature of 80 °C. Then 7 ml of ammonia (25–28 %) was added dropwise to the mixture. The mixture was placed on a stirrer for 24 h at a temperature of 80 °C. The reaction was completed overnight until a homogeneous brown-yellowish mixture was formed. The homogeneous mixture was dried in an oven, and the resulting particles (GQD) were stored [29].

This part aims to prepare MMSNs to be capped by GQDs and prevent drug leakage. 0.2 g of MMSN, 200 μl of acetic acid, and 50 μl of APTES were added to 20 ml ethanol and stirred for 24 h at room temperature. After washing the final product three times with ethanol, the produced MMSN-NH2 was dried in an oven [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: REGO loading and GQD capping on MMSNs (MMSN-REGO/GQD)
ce:section.ce:para: First, 10 mg of REGO and 6 ml of DMF were prepared. 40 mg of MMSN-NH2 was added to the solution and incubated for 24 h at 200 rpm in the dark condition. After 24 h, the MMSN-NH2 particles loaded with the drug were separated by a magnet, and the supernatant was stored to determine the amount of drug loading. The loaded MMSN-NH2 particles were sonicated and washed twice with PBS solution (pH = 7.4) to remove the absorbed drug on the surface of the particles and not in its pores [30].

For GQD capping, 10 mg of GQDs were activated by 10 mg of EDC and 5 mg of NHS in 10 ml of deionized water for 1 h 20 mg of MMSN-NH2 was slowly added to the activated GQD solution, and the reaction mixture was incubated overnight at 4 °C and 200 rpm in the dark condition. The final product was separated by the magnet and washed 5 times with deionized water to remove excess EDC, NHS, and GQD. The resulting MMSN/GQD nanoparticles were dried with a freeze dryer and stored at 4 °C [23,31].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed for three samples of Fe3O4 NPs, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD by a Philips PW1730. To investigate the magnetic behavior of Fe3O4 NPs, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD, vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) was performed by an MDKB device. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods were used to measure the porosity characteristics of MMSN, such as specific surface area, pore size distribution, and pore volume using BELSORP MINI II. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed in the region of 400–4000 cm−1 for different steps of system synthesis with FTIR RAYLEIGH WQF-510A device. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential were investigated for Fe3O4 NPs, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD using Malvern Zetasizer. To find out the thermal behavior of the nanocarrier, thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was done in the temperature range of 25–700 °C and with a heating rate of 20 °C/min for three samples of Fe3O4 NPs, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD in a neutral argon atmosphere and by TA Q600 device. TESCAN MIRA III and HITACHI S4160 field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) were used to observe and examine the surface of the nanoparticles on Fe3O4 NPs, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were prepared for MMSN and MMSN/GQD using a PHILIPS CM300 device. PG Instruments Ltd T70 UV/VIS Spectrometer was used to check the amount of drug loading and release profile.

The ninhydrin test was used to qualitatively observe the conjugation of the –NH2 group to the nanoparticles. Ninhydrin is an inorganic compound used to detect ammonia and amine in solutions. When this compound interacts with amine, the color of its derivatives changes to blue or purple. To prepare the samples, first, the MMSN and MMSN-NH2 solutions were sonicated well so that the particles were entirely homogeneously dispersed in the solution. Then, two microtubes were prepared with 0.5 ml of each solution and another solution containing 100 mg of ninhydrin in 5 ml of ethanol. Two vials were centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was drained. 0.5 ml of ninhydrin solution in ethanol was added to each sample and heated to 100 °C for 5 min. The cooled samples were measured by UV–Vis Spectrophotometer at 570 nm.

To investigate the release of REGO from the MMSN-REGO/GQD system, 30 mg of REGO-MMSN-NH2 and MMSN-REGO/GQD nanoparticles were transferred in two separate containers in 60 ml of the phosphate buffer as release mediums with 0.5 % w/v Tween80 with pHs of 1.2 and 7.4 and stirred continuously in the dark condition at 37 °C. At specific times, 500 μl of the supernatant solution was taken, and after centrifugation, it was analyzed by UV–Vis spectroscopy to determine the amount of REGO released. The loading efficiency and the loading capacity were calculated by Eq. (1) and Eq. (2) [32].
(Eq.1)
Loading
efficiency =
m a s s
o f
l o a d e d
d r u g
t o t a l
m a s s
o f
d r u g
g i v e n
i n
s o a k i n g
m e d i a
x
100
(Eq.2)
loading
capacity =
mass
of
loaded
drug
mass
of
carrier
500 μl media solution with a specific pH was added to the release medium to keep the volume constant.

Cell culture: CRC SW-480 is an epithelial cell line that can be easily attached to the plate, has a high proliferation rate, and shows sufficient detection capability. SW-480 cells were cultured in DMEM/F-12 basic medium to 89 % confluency and enriched with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % Pen/Strep antibiotic. The cells were incubated at 37 °C and in an atmosphere containing 5 % CO2 and appropriate humidity for proper cultivation.

In vitro Cytotoxicity for MMSN-REGO/GQD was investigated by MTT assay. First, SW-480 cells were cultured in a 96-well plate with a concentration of 104 cells per well. After 12 h incubation and removing the culture medium, pure REGO, and MMSN-REGO/GQD solutions in the culture medium were added to the wells. The final REGO concentrations were 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 25, 50, and 100 μg/ml, and the final volume of culture medium in each well was 100 μl. The concentrations of MMSN-REGO/GQD nanoparticles were determined according to the REGO concentration and the loading percentage. After exposing the cells to REGO and incubating for 48 h, the medium-drug solution was drained, and the MTT solution in the medium was added to the wells. The cells were exposed to MTT for 3–4 h, and then their absorbance was read at 570 nm by ELISA Microplate Reader. Cytotoxicity was expressed based on the percentage of cell viability compared to control cells without drug treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of MMSN/GQD to external magnetic field
ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was used to investigate the nanocarrier's response to the external magnetic field. The samples were dispersed evenly in an agar gel to prepare nanoparticles for imaging. For this, three stock solutions of nanoparticles were prepared. In separate microtubes, 2 mg of Fe3O4 NPs, 4 mg of MMSN, and 4.5 mg of MMSN/GQD were added to 1 ml of 10 mg/ml agar solution. After homogenization with an ultrasonic bath, 300 μl of each solution was added to 1200 μl of pure agar solution so that the concentrations of 400 μg/ml Fe3O4 NPs, 800 μg/ml MMSN and 900 μg/ml MMSN/GQD were formed. Then, 750 μl was taken from each sample and added to 750 μl of sterile agar solution to obtain different concentrations of nanoparticles. The microtubes were placed in a hot water container to boil the agar solution. After that, with an ice bath and vigorous shaking of the microtubes, a homogeneous and uniform solution was formed before the agar solidified. The different concentrations from each sample were such that the amount of Fe3O4 NPs in all three components was the same. Finally, the response of the models to the external field for Fe3O4 NPs, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD was determined by the Siemens MAGNETOM Prisma MRI Scanner at constant TR = 1500 and different TE values.
Fig. 2 shows a general schematic of the steps of synthesis, drug loading, and surface functionalization of the MMSN-REGO/GQD.

Fe3O4 NPs were synthesized using Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions, and the silica nanoparticles were coated around them using CTAB. The pores of the silica layer were created by removing the surfactant to form MMSN. After functionalizing with amine groups and REGO loading, activated GQDs by carboxyl groups were attached to the nanoparticles with the aim of capping and preventing drug leakage, forming MMSN-REGO/GQD. However, due to the weak electrostatic and hydrogen interaction between nanoparticles and GQDs, this bond is easily broken in acidic conditions, and drug molecules can quickly release from the pores. In the synthesis steps, nanoparticles without drug loading (MMSN/GQD) were also produced to check the drug behavior in different conditions.

FESEM images were used to check the morphology of nanoparticles and ensure their synthesis. From the images for Fe3O4, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD (Fig. 3 ), it was concluded that the particles were spherical in all steps of synthesis and showed an average size of 90 nm, 125 nm, and 140 nm, respectively. However, the diameter of the nanoparticles was obtained approximately, and it was observed that the synthesis was accompanied by an increase in the diameter of the particles in each step.

TEM images were used to examine the morphology of nanoparticles and the final coated surface. In the image of MMSN nanoparticles (Fig. 3D), the spherical shape of nanoparticles and core-shell structure were visible. The approximate diameter of the nanoparticles was estimated to be 120–150 nm, and the magnetic core had an approximate diameter of 100–130 nm. In the MMSN/GQD image (Fig. 3E), the capped nanoparticle was placed next to the uncapped one, and the spherical structure and GQDs placed on the surface were evident. The approximate diameter of GQDs was about 30–50 nm.

The BET analysis was performed to evaluate the specific surface area of nanoparticles, the diameter of the pores, and the shape of the porosity through the adsorption and desorption of a layer of nitrogen on the system (Fig. 4 A). According to the data measured based on the adsorption theory, the specific surface area of nanoparticles after being coated by silica and removing CTAB at 77 K was 475.82 m2/g, which is a significant amount for drug loading. Also, the average diameter of the pores was reported to be 4.35 nm, which indicates that the nanoparticles are mesoporous.

N2 adsorption and desorption diagrams represented the type IV isotherm, so the pores were mesostructured. Using the BJH curve, it was determined that the pore size distribution was almost uniform and from 2 to 4 nm. (Fig. 4B). Table 1 shows a summary of the measured values.

The values of hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of the nanoparticles after each step were summarized in Table 2 . Accordingly, it was observed that during the synthesis process, the sample size was increased. The average hydrodynamic diameter for Fe3O4, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD was 102.2 nm, 274.4 nm, and 312.6 nm, respectively.

DLS was used only to approximate the diameter of nanoparticles in each step, and the results did not necessarily match the actual values; because the DLS analysis calculated the water-covered (hydrodynamic) diameter of the particles and on the other hand, the synthesized nanoparticles had a strong tendency to accumulate and precipitate due to their magnetic properties. Also, GQDs in the final sample caused interference in light scattering and diameter measurement. Therefore, the larger diameter obtained from DLS results was predictable, compared to the size of particles observed from FESEM and TEM analysis. The advantage of this method is its non-invasiveness, as the nanoparticles were analyzed in a liquid environment, and there was no need to dry or stabilize them.

According to Table 2, the zeta potential of Fe3O4 and MMSN was measured as −39.9 mV and −15.3 mV, respectively. The reason was the presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of water-coated nanoparticles. Contrary to the fact that the GQDs also had a negative charge, the zeta potential of the MMSN/GQD was measured to be 4.7 mV. It was due to the presence of amide groups after the addition of APTES with the potential to induce a positive charge during the synthesis process. Zeta potential changes during the synthesis can also confirm the synthesis of the final nanoparticle.

The XRD pattern for Fe3O4, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 5 A. By comparing the relative intensity and position of the reflection peaks of Fe3O4 NPs with other nanoparticles, it was found that the crystal structure of iron nanoparticles did not change after silica coating and GQD capping. After attaching the GQDs, no detectable peak appeared, indicating that the MMSN-NH2 prevented the re-aggregation of most of the attached GQDs, which is similar to the observation reported by Huang et al. [33,34].

In the FTIR spectra for Fe3O4 (Fig. 5B), two small bonds were observed in a separate peak less than 1000 cm−1 and specifically around 580 cm−1, the common feature of all ferrite compounds and related to the bending vibration of Fe–O. During the synthesis process of Fe3O4 by the chemical coprecipitation method, their surfaces are easily covered with the hydroxyl groups of the aqueous medium; Therefore, the peaks observed at 1630 cm−1 and 3405 cm−1 indicated the presence of –OH groups in the sample. Due to the application of oleic acid on iron nanoparticles, peaks at 2852 cm−1 and 2922 cm−1 were observed for the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of the C–H bond in –CH2 groups, respectively; Also, due to the interference of the peaks of 1409 cm−1 related to the –CH3 group and 1710 cm−1 associated with the stretching vibration of the CO bond of oleic acid with the peak of 1630 cm−1 related to the –OH group of iron nanoparticles, disturbances were found in the range of 1300–1600 cm−1.

In the FTIR spectra related to MMSN, strong absorption areas around 1080 cm−1 and three weak peaks around 945 cm−1, 800 cm−1, and 447 cm−1 were respectively attributed to asymmetric stretching, symmetric stretching, bending mode, and the planar vibration of Si–O–Si groups, which proved the coating of silica on Fe3O4 NPs.

The stretching vibration of C–N groups at the peaks of 1340 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 and the bending vibration of N–H groups at the peaks of 1492 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 were observed in the FTIR spectrum of MMSN-NH2, which shows successful attachment of amino groups on MMSN nanoparticles.

In the GQD spectra, two broad peaks appeared in the 3000-3235 cm−1 range for the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H bonds, respectively. The stretching vibration of the CO bond in carboxyl groups was detected at 1723 cm−1, and the stretching vibration of the CC bond was detected at 1618 cm−1. The peaks at 1440 cm−1 and 1300 cm−1 indicated the bending vibration of N–H and O–H bonds, and the peak seen at 1109 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of the C–N bond. Therefore, the FTIR spectra showed the presence of different functional groups with oxygen and nitrogen in the GQD structure, and the synthesis was confirmed. Also, the existence of a CO bond in the structure of GQD increased the reaction potential with the amine group.

By comparing MMSN/GQD with MMSN-NH2, it was found that a new stretching vibration for the –CONH– bond was created at 1580 cm−1, and a distinct peak at 1725 cm−1 for the stretching vibration of the CO bond in the carbonyl group of GQDs disappeared. This phenomenon can prove the reaction of the carboxyl group of GQDs with the amine group and the formation of MMSN/GQD and MMSN-REGO/GQD.

These results are in agreement with previous studies that have used FTIR analysis to characterize similar samples. for instance, Rahimnia et al. reported similar peaks in their FTIR spectra of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [35]. Mobinikhaledi et al. observed comparable peaks in their spectra of MMSN [36]. Yao et al. identified similar peaks in their spectra of MMSN-NH2 [33]. Ghanbari et al. and Yao et al. detected corresponding peaks in their FTIR spectra of GQD [37,38]. Yao et al. also compared MMSN/GQD peaks with MMSN-NH2 in two separate articles [33,38].

Fig. 6 A shows the TGA diagrams for Fe3O4, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD. The reduced weight for each sample before 100 °C showed the mass of water evaporation, which is physically absorbed by the sample. This amount was 2 % for Fe3O4, 10 % for MMSN, and 6 % for MMSN/GQD. According to similar articles, the weight loss of Fe3O4 NPs is negligible, and the downward slope of this graph is mainly related to oleic acid, which is added to make the iron sample hydrophobic. Therefore, the 6 % weight loss of Fe3O4 indicated a thin layer of oleic acid on the surface of the nanoparticles. MMSN started to degrade at approximately 200 °C, and there was a weight loss of about 6 % up to 700 °C related to the silica coating on the nanoparticles. Furthermore, MMSN/GQD lost about 10 % weight from 200 to 700 °C; if 6 % of it is considered for silica coating, it was about 4 % weight loss related to GQDs. Table 3 shows the amount of weight loss in each sample.

Fig. 6B shows the hysteresis curve for Fe3O4, MMSN, and MMSN/GQD. These curves were drawn in −10,000 to 10,000 emu at room temperature. Since they had almost passed through the coordinate origin, the remanence and coercivity were considered to be practically zero, so these nanoparticles are superparamagnetic at ambient temperature. It was also observed that the magnetic properties of the system decreased by coating Fe3O4 NPs with silica and GQDs.

The maximum measured value of the saturation magnetization (SM) at ambient temperature was related to Fe3O4, which showed 49.03 emu.g−1. Generally, the SM for iron nanoparticles is around 60–80 emu/g. Since the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were coated with oleic acid, the calculated value seemed reasonable. After coating with silica, this number decreased to 20.02 emu.g−1, which was due to the increase in the non-magnetic mass, the decrease in the relative abundance of iron nanoparticles, and as a result, the decrease in the magnetic property of the system. It was observed that the MMSN/GQD had a tiny hysteresis loop with a coercivity of 83.11 Oe and a remanence of 2.64 emu.g−1, and the SM was estimated to be 14.93 emu.g−1. Therefore, the final nanoparticles can produce heat to create magnetic hyperthermia [33]. The amount of magnetic properties of the nanoparticles during the synthesis is briefly reported in Table 4 .

These results are comparable to previous studies. For example, Yao et al. reported the MS value of 50 emu.g−1 for iron nanoparticles with oleic acid [33]. Esmi et al. and Yin et al. also measured the MS values of about 20 emu.g−1 for MMSN and MMSN/GQD [27,39]. This comparison shows that the nanoparticles synthesized in this project were acceptable regarding magnetism and response to the external field.

Fig. 7 is the pictures of prepared solutions before and after adding ninhydrin. In Fig. 7A, samples (a) and (b) are MMSN-NH2 and MMSN before the test, respectively. Samples (c), (d), and (e) in Fig. 7B represent MMSN-NH2, MMSN, and ninhydrin solution in ethanol after the reaction. Only the color of the solution containing MMSN-NH2 changed, and this phenomenon proved the presence of the amine groups in these nanoparticles.

REGO was used as a hydrophobic drug to investigate the loading capacity and release ability of the nanoparticles. The drug loaded into the MMSN-REGO/GQD was calculated using UV–Vis spectroscopy at λmax = 271 nm for REGO. The amount of unloaded REGO in the supernatant solution was 6.9 mg. As a result, the loading efficiency and the loading capacity were 31 % and 77.5 μg mg−1, respectively. Yao et al. and Gao et al. reported the loading properties for the same nanoparticles as 47 μg mg−1 and 8.1 % for the hydrophilic drug [23,33]. According to these results, the loading amount of REGO was acceptable.

Drug release studies were conducted to examine the impact of pH fluctuations and GQDs on controlling REGO release. For this purpose, two REGO-MMSN-NH2 and MMSN-REGO/GQD were incubated at pH 1.2 and 7.4 at 37 °C and 200 rpm. The UV-VIS spectroscopy was used to measure the absorption of the supernatants. As shown in Fig. 8 , MMSN-REGO/GQD had a maximum release of 50 % and 14 % for pH 1.2 and 7.4, respectively. As for REGO-MMSN-NH2, the maximum release was 80 % and 20 %, respectively. This indicated the possibility of weakening the electrostatic interaction between MSN and GQD under acidic conditions, leading to the further release of REGO. The amount of REGO released at pH = 7.4 from REGO-MMSN-NH2 and MMSN-REGO/GQD was minimal, but this was significant at pH = 1.2. Therefore, the drug release from the MMSN/GQD increases as the pH of the environment decreases.

The effects of MMSN-REGO/GQD on CRC SW-480 cell culture were studied using the MTT assay. The results, as shown in Fig. 9 , indicated that the MMSN/GQD carrier did not exhibit any cytotoxicity, even at the highest concentration. However, the cell viability was slightly affected by the different concentrations of REGO, resulting in a decrease from 85 % to 57 %. Remarkably, the survival rate was significantly reduced from 69 % to 11 % by MMSN-REGO/GQD. The sudden drop in cell viability at 25, 50, and 100 μg ml−1 concentrations of MMSN-REGO/GQD was due to the weight of nanoparticles suffocating the cells. However, for other concentrations, the cell viability of MMSN-REGO/GQD was significantly reduced compared to free REGO. By curve fitting the cell viability diagrams for both MMSN-REGO/GQD and free REGO, the half-maximum inhibitory concentration (IC50) was calculated to be 85.61 μg.ml-1 for free REGO and 6.293 μg ml−1 for the drug-loaded nanoparticles. These findings suggest that MMSN-REGO/GQD caused more cell death than free REGO, highlighting the benefits of using nanoparticles for hydrophobic drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of MMSN/GQD to external magnetic field
ce:section.ce:para: MRI was conducted to investigate the response of the MMSN/GQD to the external magnetic field. The nanoparticles produced a contrasting effect by influencing the relaxation time (T1, T2) of the surrounding environment, resulting in a darkened domain (water was utilized in this experiment).

Superparamagnetic iron nanoparticles have a transversal relaxation time (T2), which can be calculated using the signal intensity relationship regarding the applied external field (Eq. (3)).
(Eq.3)
Signal
(
TE ,
T 2
)
= S .
e
TE
T 2
In order to calculate the relaxation time, both the signal intensity and external field are required. The contrast intensity of each microtube was measured using a contrast detection software, and the TE-signal intensity curve was drawn for different concentrations (Fig. 10 ). By applying an exponential decay function on each diagram, a function in the form of Eq. (3) was obtained. T2 was calculated through curve fitting for each concentration (Fig. 11 A). According to the relaxation times (T2) calculated for different concentrations of the nanoparticles, it was clear that the smaller the nanoparticles, the longer the collapse time. So, more time was required for the magnetic vector to increase in the transversal plates. Moreover, it was clear that with the increase in the coating of magnetic nanoparticles, the relaxation time was increased, which means that the magnetic properties of the particles will decrease (Fig. 11A). Generally, it was concluded that the transversal relaxation time is the lowest for Fe3O4 NPs and the highest for MMSN/GQD.

The transversal relaxivity coefficient (r2) was determined by plotting the inverse of the transversal relaxation time (1/T2) as a function of nanoparticle concentrations. The slope of the linear regression line was taken as the r2 value under the experimental conditions. The highest magnetic response, as shown in Fig. 11B, was exhibited by Fe3O4 nanoparticles, with an r2 value of 0.1482, which decreased as the coated layers were increased. Furthermore, the r2 values for MMSN and MMSN/GQD nanoparticles were 0.0873 and 0.0804, respectively. The decreasing behavior of relaxivity coefficients was investigated by Ye et al. in relation to the thickness and the porosity of the silica layer [40].

Finally, it can be inferred that the synthesized nanoparticles possess magnetic properties and could be used as a diagnostic system in high concentrations, tracked by MRI, and even navigated under external magnetic fields. However, further in vivo studies are necessary to confirm their efficacy.

In this research, a multifunctional nanocarrier, MMSN/GQD nanoparticle, with the potential of theranostic application, was introduced. The release of the REGO as an anticancer drug from the MMSN-REGO/GQD was pH-dependent and controllable. The interaction between GQDs and MSN was broken in acidic pH, leading to an increase in the release rate. GQDs also functioned as an agent to reduce REGO leakage. In therapeutic tests conducted on the colorectal cancer SW-480 cell line, it was found that loading REGO in MMSN/GQD demonstrated efficient anticancer activity while maintaining biocompatible. In diagnostic analysis, it was discovered that the MMSN/GQD could be navigated and performed online imaging at high concentrations. Moreover, the potential of nanoparticles for hyperthermia was also determined. Therefore, MMSN/GQD nanoparticles can be a promising multifunctional and theragnostic system in the treatment of colorectal cancer.

Seyyed Mostafa Hojjati: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Zeinab Salehi: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mohammad Akrami: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
